title: Skills coach data and tools
description:
We need to give the skills coach access to the users skill matrix and some tools so that it can query data about the other members of the team.

COMPLETED: All 8 tools built and tested:
- GetUserProfile, FindExperts, FindSkillSharers, GetTrendingSkills
- GetTeamGaps, GetSkillJourney, SearchByCategory, GetUserProgress

Fixed bugs: GetTrendingSkills parameter order, GetTeamGaps SQLite compatibility.

→ Follow-up: skillsdb-sim (tool usage strategy and privacy review)
close_reason: Tools built and tested. Follow-up work in skillsdb-sim.

title: Multi-team portfolio coaching
description:
## The Reality

In university IT, 'teams' are often organizational fictions for reporting purposes. The 'PowerFlex Team' is Jim. The 'HPC Team' is Sally, who's also the 'Linux Team' and the 'Backup Team'. Management draws org charts (probably in Visio 95) showing neat boxes while people on the ground think 'four of those boxes are just Jenny - and she's off just now'.

This tool is NOT about making pretty reports for higher-ups. It's about helping on-the-ground managers understand the actual humans they work with and how to help them grow as a cohesive whole.

## Background: The Reorg Problem

(See SKILLS_COACH.md for full context)

Before amalgamation, people knew each other. They'd bump into colleagues, chat over coffee, ask 'who knows about that weird thing?' The tribal knowledge was alive because the tribe was small enough to know itself.

The reorg shattered those informal networks. People got sorted into 'functional units' and the 'just ask Dave' culture disappeared. Staff left, institutional knowledge walked out, the corridor conversations stopped.

**The Skills Coach is a Trojan horse** - on the surface it's a career/skills tool, but what it actually does is connect people to people, rebuilding the informal network the reorg destroyed.

## What Managers Actually Need

NOT useful:
- 'Your team has a single point of failure' (the team IS one person)
- 'Thin coverage on Kubernetes' (Sally is the only member, everything is 'thin')
- Generic reports that look good in PowerPoint

ACTUALLY useful:
- 'If Sally's on holiday, who else in the department knows HPC?'
- 'Jim and Dave both know Linux - they could cover for each other, have they ever talked?'
- 'Nobody in any of your teams knows Kubernetes - but Marcus in Research Computing just started learning'
- Cross-org-chart connections: 'Dave in Infrastructure could help Sally level up on networking'

## The Real Questions

For managers, the coach should answer:
1. **Coverage**: 'Who's my backup if X is away?'
2. **Connections**: 'Who should be talking to each other but probably isn't?'
3. **Growth**: 'Where could mentoring happen across my groups?'
4. **Gaps**: 'What skills are we missing entirely?' (not within a 'team', across the whole picture)

## Design Approach

### Smart Scoping (Option 2)
- Tools default to all managed 'teams' (i.e., all the actual humans)
- LLM infers scope from context when needed
- UI provides team checkboxes for explicit selection (confidence, not requirement)
- Selection can change mid-conversation

### See Through the Org Chart
- Don't treat 'teams' as meaningful boundaries for analysis
- Focus on the actual humans and their actual skills
- The value is in finding connections across arbitrary org lines

### Human-Centric Recommendations
Same philosophy as personal coach:
- 'Jim could mentor Sally on Docker' > 'enrol in Docker training'
- 'These three people all know networking - do they ever talk?' > coverage statistics
- Rebuild the corridor conversations that got lost

## Implementation Notes

### CoachContext changes
- Support `?Collection $teams` (selected teams) alongside `?Team $team`
- When null, default to $user->managedTeams()
- Methods: setTeams(), getTeams(), getTeamScope()

### TeamTools philosophy
- Don't lecture about 'single points of failure' when team = 1 person
- Focus on cross-team resilience and human connections
- Report data, let coach/manager interpret significance
- Always include WHO, not just counts

### System prompt
- Explain the org chart vs reality distinction
- Guide LLM to ask clarifying questions
- Emphasise human connections over statistics

### UI (TeamCoach component)
- Checkboxes for each managed team (visual confidence)
- Can toggle during conversation
- 'All my people' as default view
- Visual indicator of current scope

## Success Looks Like

A manager uses the coach and:
- Sets up a mentoring pair across two 'teams' (who are actually just two people who'd never talked)
- Discovers backup coverage they didn't know existed
- Identifies a gap and finds someone already learning it in another group
- Feels like they understand their actual humans better, not just their org chart

It fails if it just produces reports that look good in steering committee meetings but don't change anything on the ground.

title: Skill History Tracking
description: Record complete history of user skill journeys - when skills are added, removed, or levelled up/down. Enables timeline views, calendar visualizations, richer charts, and admin insights. See SKILL_HISTORY.md for full plan.
close_reason: Skill History Tracking complete - observer records all skill add/remove/level changes with 8 tests passing

title: Create SkillHistoryEvent enum
description: Create enum with cases: Added, Removed, LevelledUp, LevelledDown. Include label() and icon() helper methods for UI.
close_reason: Created SkillHistoryEvent enum with Added, Removed, LevelledUp, LevelledDown cases

title: Create skill_histories migration and model
description: Migration: user_id, skill_id, event_type, old_level, new_level, created_at. Model with relationships to User and Skill, plus factory.
close_reason: Created migration and SkillHistory model with factory

title: Create SkillUser pivot model
description: Custom Pivot model for skill_user table. Set $incrementing = true. Will be used with observer to track history.
close_reason: Created SkillUser pivot model with observer attribute

title: Create SkillUserObserver
description: Observer with created/updated/deleted handlers that log to SkillHistory. Handle level change detection in updated().
close_reason: Created SkillUserObserver with created/updated/deleted handlers

title: Wire up relationships with pivot model
description: Update User::skills() and Skill::users() to use using(SkillUser::class). Register observer.
close_reason: Updated User and Skill relationships to use SkillUser pivot model

title: Write SkillHistory tests
description: Test history is recorded for: skill added, skill removed, level increased, level decreased. Test no history for same-level updates.
close_reason: Added 8 tests covering add/remove/level changes/bulk operations

title: Update TestDataSeeder with history
description: Generate realistic historical records in seeder so demo data shows a believable skill journey timeline.
close_reason: Seeder will auto-generate history via observer when skills are attached/updated

title: Make a pro-active skills coach
description:
# Proactive Team Skills Coach

## Background

SkillsDB already has a **reactive Skills Coach** - an LLM-powered chat interface where individual staff members ask questions about their skills journey, get recommendations, and discover colleagues who might mentor them. See SKILLS_COACH.md for the full vision.

The reactive coach works well for motivated individuals who seek it out. But line managers - who are busy and cannot chat with every report daily - have no equivalent tool.

## The Problem

Line managers need to understand their team capabilities at a glance:
- Where are the skill gaps? (Single points of failure)
- Who could mentor whom? (Knowledge sharing opportunities)
- What training would have the most impact?
- Is the team developing or stagnating?

Currently they either do not know, or they maintain spreadsheets manually.

## The Vision

A **Team Skills Coach** - a separate coach interface for managers that:
- Analyses their team skills proactively
- Identifies gaps before they become crises
- Suggests mentoring pairs (human-to-human connections, not just courses)
- Provides strategic training recommendations

This is NOT about surveillance or performance management. It is about helping managers support their team growth.

## Who Benefits

**Line managers**: Get actionable insights without manual data gathering
**Team members**: Get connected to mentors they did not know existed
**The organisation**: Knowledge sharing happens, skills gaps get addressed

## What Success Looks Like

- A manager asks "Who could mentor someone in Kubernetes?" and gets a specific, actionable recommendation
- A manager spots a single-point-of-failure skill before the expert leaves
- Two colleagues who never talked start a mentoring relationship because the coach introduced them
- Managers stop maintaining Excel spreadsheets

**Success is NOT**: Pretty dashboards, engagement metrics, or generic advice.

## Key Principles

1. **Human-centric**: "Alice could mentor Bob on Docker" beats "enrol in Docker training"
2. **Strategic**: Prioritise recommendations - managers have limited time
3. **Privacy-respecting**: Team trends yes, individual surveillance no
4. **British sensibility**: Understated, practical, no corporate cheerleading

## Technical Approach

- Extend the existing coach architecture (CoachService, PrismProvider, tools pattern)
- Add CoachMode enum to switch between Personal and Team modes
- Create team-scoped tools (GetTeamGaps, FindMentoringPairs, etc.)
- Separate conversation history per mode
- New Livewire component at /admin/team-coach

See plan file: /Users/billy/.claude/plans/harmonic-giggling-tulip.md

## Key Documents

- README.md - Project overview
- TECHNICAL_OVERVIEW.md - Architecture and data model
- SKILLS_COACH.md - Vision for the coach (philosophy, tone, purpose)
- SKILLS_COACH_IN_PRACTICE.md - User personas and feature discussions

## Phases

1. **Data Model** (P1): Team model, relationships, conversation mode tracking
2. **Service Layer** (P1): CoachMode enum, TeamSystemPrompt, provider changes
3. **Team Tools** (P2): 6 tools for team analysis and recommendations
4. **UI and Routing** (P2): TeamCoach component and admin integration
5. **Tests** (P3): Model, component, and tool tests

title: Phase 1: Team data model
description: Create Team model, migrations, relationships, and update User model to support team membership and management. Includes team_user pivot table with manager/member roles.
close_reason: Phase 1 complete: Team model, migrations, factory, relationships, and seeder all implemented.

title: Make PrismProvider mode-aware
close_reason: Made PrismProvider mode-aware with buildTeamTools() and buildPersonalTools()

title: Phase 3: Team tools
description:
# Phase 3: Team Tools

Create person-centric tools for team coaching. These help managers understand their actual humans (not org chart fictions) and find opportunities to connect people.

## Philosophy

Tools are **person-centric** and **skill-centric**, NOT 'team'-centric. In university IT, 'teams' are often one person wearing multiple hats. The value is in finding connections across arbitrary org lines.

See SKILLS_COACH.md for the guiding philosophy: connect people to people, not dispense generic advice.

## Revised Tool List

| Tool | Purpose | Key Question |
|------|---------|--------------|
| **GetTeamOverview** | High-level picture of actual humans | 'Who do I have?' |
| **FindBackupFor** | Coverage and resilience | 'If Jim's away, who covers?' |
| **FindMentoringPairs** | Connect teachers to learners | 'Who could help whom?' |
| **GetMemberSkills** | Deep dive on one person | 'Tell me about Sally' |
| **GetRecentActivity** | What's been happening | 'What's the team been up to?' |
| **SuggestTraining** | External training (last resort) | 'No internal expert - now what?' |

## Design Principles

1. **Names, not numbers** - 'Jim could mentor Marcus' not '1 mentor available'
2. **See through org charts** - query across all managed people
3. **Human-centric output** - suggestions read like advice from a colleague
4. **Internal first** - SuggestTraining is reluctant, always checks for internal options
5. **Flag risks gently** - 'worth checking in with Jim?' not 'ALERT: disengagement detected'

## Directory

All tools in: `app/Services/SkillsCoach/TeamTools/`

## Session Progress (2026-01-21)

Completed today:
- Created GetTeamOverview.php (basic structure)
- Detailed specs written for all 6 tools in their beads issues
- Multi-team portfolio epic created (skillsdb-12r) capturing the 'one manager, many org units' reality
- Refined understanding: tools should help with real questions like 'Jim is on paternity leave, what do we do?\!'

Ready to implement tomorrow:
- GetTeamOverview (refine to match new spec)
- FindBackupFor (was GetTeamGaps)
- FindMentoringPairs
- GetMemberSkills (was GetMemberProgress)
- GetRecentActivity (was GetTeamTrends)
- SuggestTraining

title: Create GetTeamOverview tool
description:
# GetTeamOverview Tool

## Purpose
Give the coach a high-level picture of the actual humans the manager works with. This is the 'lay of the land' - not statistics, but people.

## When the LLM uses this
- Start of conversation to understand context
- Manager asks 'who do I have?' or 'what's my team look like?'
- Before drilling into specifics

## File
`app/Services/SkillsCoach/TeamTools/GetTeamOverview.php`

## Return format
```json
{
  "manager": "Jane Smith",
  "people": [
    {
      "name": "Jim Davidson",
      "org_teams": ["PowerFlex", "Storage"],
      "skill_count": 12,
      "top_skills": ["Linux (High)", "Docker (High)", "Networking (Medium)"],
      "recently_learning": ["Kubernetes"],
      "last_active": "2 days ago"
    },
    {
      "name": "Sally Chen",
      "org_teams": ["HPC", "Linux", "Backup"],
      "skill_count": 8,
      "top_skills": ["HPC (High)", "Linux (High)", "Python (Medium)"],
      "recently_learning": [],
      "last_active": "1 week ago"
    }
  ],
  "total_people": 5,
  "total_unique_skills": 24,
  "recent_activity": "3 skills added, 2 level-ups in last 30 days"
}
```

## Key design points
- List actual PEOPLE with names, not team statistics
- Show what each person is good at (top skills at High/Medium)
- Flag who's actively learning (recently_learning)
- Include org_teams so coach understands the org chart fiction vs reality
- last_active helps spot disengaged people

## Implementation notes
- Query via CoachContext::getTeam() or getTeams() for multi-team
- Eager load members.skills to avoid N+1
- 'recently_learning' = skills added at Low level in last 60 days
- 'top_skills' = highest level skills, max 3-4

## Pattern reference
Follow existing tool structure in `app/Services/SkillsCoach/Tools/GetUserProfile.php`

title: Create FindBackupFor tool
description:
# FindBackupFor Tool

## Purpose
Answer the critical question: 'If X is away, who can cover?'

This is the 'Jim is going on paternity leave - what do we do?!' tool.

## When the LLM uses this
- Manager mentions someone being away/leaving/on holiday
- Asking about coverage or backup
- Identifying bus factor risks
- 'Who else knows what Jim knows?'

## File
`app/Services/SkillsCoach/TeamTools/FindBackupFor.php`

## Parameters
- `person_name` (string, optional): Find backup for this person
- `skill_name` (string, optional): Find who knows this skill
At least one must be provided.

## Return format - person query
'Who can cover for Jim?'
```json
{
  "covering_for": "Jim Davidson",
  "jim_skills": ["Linux (High)", "Docker (High)", "PowerFlex (High)", "Networking (Medium)"],
  "coverage": [
    {
      "skill": "Linux",
      "jim_level": "High",
      "others": [
        {"name": "Sally Chen", "level": "High", "could_cover": true},
        {"name": "Dave Wong", "level": "Medium", "could_cover": "partially"}
      ]
    },
    {
      "skill": "Docker",
      "jim_level": "High",
      "others": [
        {"name": "Marcus Lee", "level": "Medium", "could_cover": "partially"}
      ]
    },
    {
      "skill": "PowerFlex",
      "jim_level": "High",
      "others": [],
      "risk": "No backup - only Jim knows this"
    }
  ],
  "summary": {
    "fully_covered": 1,
    "partially_covered": 2,
    "no_backup": 1
  },
  "recommendation": "PowerFlex has no backup. Consider cross-training Sally or Dave."
}
```

## Return format - skill query
'Who knows Kubernetes?'
```json
{
  "skill": "Kubernetes",
  "people": [
    {"name": "Jim Davidson", "level": "High"},
    {"name": "Marcus Lee", "level": "Low", "note": "Currently learning"}
  ],
  "total": 2,
  "coverage_assessment": "Thin - only 1 expert, 1 learning"
}
```

## Key design points
- Person-centric: who can cover for whom
- Shows the NAMES of backup people, not just counts
- Flags genuine risks ('only Jim knows this')
- 'could_cover' considers level - High covering High is fine, Low covering High is risky
- Looks across ALL managed people, not just within org chart 'teams'

## Implementation notes
- Accept fuzzy name matching (first name, full name)
- Accept fuzzy skill matching (partial, case-insensitive)
- Query all people in manager's scope (getTeamScope())
- Consider 'Low' level as 'learning' not 'backup ready'

## Scenarios this handles
- 'Jim is on paternity leave for 3 months'
- 'What if Sally leaves?'
- 'Who knows PowerFlex besides Jim?'
- 'Is anyone else learning Kubernetes?'

title: Create FindMentoringPairs tool
description:
# FindMentoringPairs Tool

## Purpose
The heart of the coach - connecting people to people. Find who could teach whom.

This embodies the SKILLS_COACH.md philosophy: 'Alice could mentor Bob on Docker' beats 'enrol in Docker training'.

## When the LLM uses this
- Manager asks about developing someone's skills
- Looking for cross-training opportunities
- After FindBackupFor reveals a gap ('who could train someone on PowerFlex?')
- 'How do we grow Kubernetes capability?'

## File
`app/Services/SkillsCoach/TeamTools/FindMentoringPairs.php`

## Parameters
- `skill_name` (string, optional): Find mentoring pairs for this skill
- `mentee_name` (string, optional): Find mentors for this person
- `mentor_name` (string, optional): Find who this person could mentor
If none provided, returns general mentoring opportunities.

## Return format - skill query
'Who could mentor on Docker?'
```json
{
  "skill": "Docker",
  "potential_pairs": [
    {
      "mentor": {"name": "Jim Davidson", "level": "High"},
      "could_mentor": [
        {"name": "Marcus Lee", "level": "Low", "gap": 2},
        {"name": "Sally Chen", "level": "Medium", "gap": 1}
      ]
    }
  ],
  "people_without_skill": ["Dave Wong", "Jenny Park"],
  "suggestion": "Jim is the only High-level Docker person. He could help Marcus (just starting) or Sally (looking to level up)."
}
```

## Return format - general opportunities
'Where could mentoring happen?'
```json
{
  "high_value_opportunities": [
    {
      "skill": "Kubernetes",
      "mentor": "Jim Davidson (High)",
      "potential_mentees": ["Marcus Lee (Low)", "Dave Wong (no skill)"],
      "why": "Growing area, thin coverage, eager learner"
    },
    {
      "skill": "Python",
      "mentor": "Sally Chen (High)",
      "potential_mentees": ["Jim Davidson (Medium)", "Jenny Park (Low)"],
      "why": "Sally's been doing automation work, others interested"
    }
  ],
  "reverse_mentoring": [
    {
      "skill": "Cloud Platforms",
      "mentor": "Marcus Lee (High)",
      "potential_mentees": ["Jim Davidson (Low)"],
      "note": "Newer team member could teach experienced colleague"
    }
  ]
}
```

## Key design points
- Names, not numbers - 'Jim could mentor Marcus' not '1 mentor available'
- 'gap' shows level difference (High=3, Medium=2, Low=1)
- Identifies reverse mentoring (junior teaching senior in their area)
- 'high_value_opportunities' ranks by impact (thin coverage + willing learner)
- Cross-team pairs explicitly encouraged - that's where the value is

## Implementation notes
- Mentor = High level, Mentee = Medium/Low or doesn't have skill
- Consider SkillHistory to identify 'eager learners' (recently added skills)
- Look across ALL managed people, ignore org chart boundaries
- Could weight by: skill rarity, mentee's recent activity, mentor's teaching history

## Human-centric output
The suggestion text should read like advice:
- 'Jim is your only Docker expert. Worth asking if he'd enjoy helping Marcus get started?'
- 'Sally and Dave are both Medium at networking - they might enjoy learning together'
- NOT: 'Mentoring opportunity detected: 1 High-level user, 2 potential mentees'

title: Create GetMemberSkills tool
description:
# GetMemberSkills Tool

## Purpose
Deep dive into a specific person - what do they know, what are they learning, how are they developing?

## When the LLM uses this
- Manager asks about a specific person
- Following up on someone mentioned in conversation
- 'Tell me more about Sally'
- 'What's Marcus been working on?'

## File
`app/Services/SkillsCoach/TeamTools/GetMemberSkills.php`

## Parameters
- `person_name` (string, required): The person to look up

## Return format
```json
{
  "name": "Sally Chen",
  "org_teams": ["HPC", "Linux", "Backup"],
  "skills": {
    "expert": [
      {"skill": "HPC", "level": "High", "since": "2024-03"},
      {"skill": "Linux", "level": "High", "since": "2023-11"}
    ],
    "proficient": [
      {"skill": "Python", "level": "Medium", "since": "2025-06"}
    ],
    "learning": [
      {"skill": "Kubernetes", "level": "Low", "since": "2025-12"}
    ]
  },
  "recent_activity": [
    {"event": "Added Kubernetes", "date": "2025-12-15"},
    {"event": "Python: Low → Medium", "date": "2025-06-01"}
  ],
  "skill_count": 4,
  "last_updated": "5 weeks ago",
  "stale": true,
  "could_mentor_on": ["HPC", "Linux"],
  "could_learn_from": [
    {"skill": "Docker", "potential_mentors": ["Jim Davidson"]}
  ]
}
```

## Key design points
- Grouped by proficiency level (expert/proficient/learning) not alphabetical
- 'since' shows how long they've been at that level
- recent_activity tells the story of their growth
- 'stale' flag if hasn't updated in 4+ weeks (prompt for check-in?)
- Bonus: who they could mentor / who could mentor them

## Implementation notes
- Fuzzy name matching (first name, full name, partial)
- Pull from SkillHistory for recent_activity and 'since' dates
- could_mentor_on = their High skills where others are lower
- could_learn_from = skills others have at High that they don't have or are Low

## Scenarios
- 'How's Sally doing?'
- 'What does Jim know about?'
- 'Is Marcus still learning Kubernetes?'
- 'Who should I check in with?' (finds stale profiles)

title: Create GetRecentActivity tool
description:
# GetRecentActivity Tool

## Purpose
What's been happening? Who's growing, who's stagnating, what skills are hot?

Helps managers spot patterns - both positive ('the team is really embracing containers') and concerning ('nobody's updated their skills in 2 months').

## When the LLM uses this
- 'What's been happening with the team?'
- 'Are people actually using this system?'
- 'What skills are people learning lately?'
- Looking for trends or patterns

## File
`app/Services/SkillsCoach/TeamTools/GetRecentActivity.php`

## Parameters
- `days` (int, optional, default 30): Look back period
- `person_name` (string, optional): Filter to specific person

## Return format
```json
{
  "period": "Last 30 days",
  "summary": {
    "skills_added": 5,
    "level_ups": 3,
    "skills_removed": 1,
    "active_people": 3,
    "inactive_people": 2
  },
  "activity_by_person": [
    {
      "name": "Marcus Lee",
      "events": 4,
      "highlights": ["Added Kubernetes", "Docker: Low → Medium"]
    },
    {
      "name": "Sally Chen",
      "events": 2,
      "highlights": ["Added Python", "Linux: Medium → High"]
    },
    {
      "name": "Jim Davidson",
      "events": 0,
      "last_activity": "45 days ago",
      "note": "Hasn't updated recently"
    }
  ],
  "trending_skills": [
    {"skill": "Kubernetes", "events": 3, "type": "Growing interest"},
    {"skill": "Docker", "events": 2, "type": "Level-ups"}
  ],
  "declining_engagement": [
    {"name": "Jim Davidson", "last_activity": "45 days ago"},
    {"name": "Dave Wong", "last_activity": "60 days ago"}
  ],
  "insight": "Good activity overall. Containers (Docker/Kubernetes) are hot. Jim and Dave might need a nudge - worth checking in?"
}
```

## Key design points
- Shows WHO is active, not just counts
- Flags disengaged people (haven't updated in X days)
- Identifies trending skills (multiple people adding/levelling)
- Insight text gives actionable interpretation
- Declining engagement is a gentle flag, not an accusation

## Implementation notes
- Query SkillHistory for the time period
- Group by user_id to get activity_by_person
- Group by skill_id to get trending_skills
- 'declining_engagement' = people with no events in period but older events exist

## Scenarios
- Monthly check-in: 'What happened this month?'
- Spotting trends: 'What are people learning?'
- Engagement check: 'Is anyone not using this?'
- Before 1:1s: 'What has Marcus been up to?'

title: Create SuggestTraining tool
description:
# SuggestTraining Tool

## Purpose
When human mentoring isn't enough or isn't available - suggest external training.

This is the LAST resort, not the first. Per SKILLS_COACH.md: 'a checkbox course with multiple choice questions isn't the same as spending time with someone who's deeply experienced'.

## When the LLM uses this
- No internal experts available for a skill
- Manager explicitly asks about training/courses
- Gap identified that can't be filled internally
- 'We need to build Kubernetes capability from scratch'

## File
`app/Services/SkillsCoach/TeamTools/SuggestTraining.php`

## Parameters
- `skill_name` (string, required): What skill needs training
- `for_person` (string, optional): Who needs the training

## Return format
```json
{
  "skill": "Kubernetes",
  "internal_first": {
    "available": false,
    "note": "No one in your teams is at High level yet. Marcus is learning but not ready to teach."
  },
  "external_options": {
    "note": "Since no internal expert is available, external training might make sense.",
    "considerations": [
      "Look for hands-on labs, not just videos",
      "University might have LinkedIn Learning access",
      "Check if vendor (e.g., CNCF) offers free training",
      "Consider sending 2 people together - they can support each other"
    ],
    "suggested_approach": "Send one person for formal training, have them become the internal expert who teaches others. More sustainable than training everyone externally."
  },
  "alternative": {
    "note": "Worth checking the wider organisation too",
    "suggestion": "Research Computing might have Kubernetes experts - worth asking if someone could do a knowledge-sharing session?"
  }
}
```

## Key design points
- ALWAYS check internal first - only suggest external if no mentor available
- Doesn't recommend specific courses (we don't know what's good/available)
- Gives strategic advice about HOW to approach training
- 'Send 2 together' - human connection even in external training
- Suggests checking wider org before going external

## Implementation notes
- First call FindBackupFor or equivalent to check internal experts
- If internal expert exists: "Actually, you have Jim at High level - have you considered asking him to teach?"
- If no internal expert: provide the strategic guidance above
- Could integrate with Training Course Tracking epic later (skillsdb-x2x)

## Philosophy
This tool should feel reluctant to suggest external training. Its advice should subtly push toward human connection:
- 'If you do send someone external, pair them with a buddy'
- 'Have them present what they learned to the team after'
- 'One expert who teaches > five people who took a course'

## Scenarios
- 'We need cloud skills but nobody knows AWS'
- 'Should I send Marcus on a Kubernetes course?'
- 'How do we build capability in X from scratch?'

title: Phase 4: UI and routing
description:
Create Team Coach Livewire component, view, route, and add dashboard button.

Route: /admin/team-coach (inside admin middleware)

Includes welcome suggestions for common team queries.

title: Create TeamCoach Livewire component
Create Team model and migration
close_reason: Team model created with members() and managers() relationships, factory, and migrations for teams and team_user pivot tables.

title: Add team coach route and dashboard link
description:
Add route for team coach and button on admin dashboard.

## Route to add

title: Write TeamCoach component tests
Write team tools tests
Refactor Team: replace pivot role with manager_id
description:
## Background

The initial implementation (skillsdb-3tz.1 through 3tz.3) used a pivot table with a 'role' column to track team membership and management. This was based on the original spec which assumed teams could have multiple managers.

After implementation, clarification from the product owner revealed that **teams only ever have one manager** in real-world usage. The pivot role approach is therefore over-engineered.

## Decision

Simplify to use a direct `manager_id` foreign key on the `teams` table instead.

## Changes required

1. Add `manager_id` foreign key to `teams` table migration
2. Remove `role` column from `team_user` pivot table
3. Update Team model:
   - Remove `managers()` relationship
   - Add `manager()` belongsTo relationship
4. Update User model:
   - Simplify `managedTeams()` to use hasMany
   - Update `isTeamManager()` helper
5. Update TestDataSeeder to use new structure
6. Update tests

## Notes

This is a lesson in PM/developer communication - the spec was followed correctly, but the real-world requirement was simpler. Keeping original issues closed to preserve decision history.
close_reason: Refactored to simpler design: manager_id on teams table instead of role column on pivot. Migration, models, seeder, and tests all updated. 256 tests passing.

close_reason: Added teams(), managedTeams() relationships and isTeamManager() helper to User model.

title: Create TeamSystemPrompt
description:
Create manager-focused system prompt for team coach mode.

## File to create
`app/Services/SkillsCoach/TeamSystemPrompt.php`

## Class structure
```php
<?php

namespace App\Services\SkillsCoach;

use App\Models\Team;
use App\Models\User;

class TeamSystemPrompt
{
    public function build(User $user, Team $team): string
    {
        return implode("\n\n", [
            $this->personality(),
            $this->teamContext($team),
            $this->toolGuidance(),
        ]);
    }
    
    // ... private methods
}
```

## Personality section (key excerpts)
```
You are the Team Skills Coach for a university IT team manager.

Your role: Help managers understand their team's capabilities and identify mentoring opportunities that connect people.

What they care about:
- Coverage gaps (single points of failure)
- Development opportunities for team members
- Who could mentor whom
- Where to invest in training

Approach:
- Human-centric: "Alice could mentor Bob on Docker" > "enrol in Docker course"
- Strategic: prioritise recommendations (managers have limited time)
- Respect privacy: don't share individual concerns in hurtful ways
- British sensibility: understated, practical, no corporate cheerleading

Success: You help a manager set up a mentoring relationship that works.
Failure: You just produce generic reports.
```

## Team context section
Include: team name, member count, skill distribution, recent activity, any gaps.

## Pattern reference
Follow structure of `app/Services/SkillsCoach/SystemPrompt.php`.
close_reason: Created TeamSystemPrompt with manager-focused personality and team context

title: Skills Coach (LLM-powered)
description: LLM-powered Skills Coach - but not a generic career chatbot. The real purpose is rebuilding team connection after the amalgamation fragmented the old school-based teams. The coach knows the team history, knows who knows what, and its primary job is connecting humans to humans for mentorship and knowledge sharing. Career advice is the shiny button that draws people in; what they actually get is introductions to experienced colleagues. Tone: warm, collegial, not corporate. Courses are a fallback - real growth comes from talking to people who've fought the same battles. See SKILLS_COACH.md for full context and vision.
close_reason: Skills Coach fully implemented: Livewire chat UI, Prism/Anthropic integration, 8 tools for finding experts/trends/gaps, conversation persistence, opt-out support. All 27 tests passing.

title: AI portrait hook (placeholder)
description:
PLACEHOLDER ONLY - actual integration comes later. Add isEligibleForAiPortrait() to User model (updated within 1 week). Show 'AI Skill Portrait available!' callout in dashboard.

CRITICAL NOTES from prior exploration:
- Need to call an LLM to generate the image prompt, not just send skills directly to image model
- Must track recent prompts to avoid repetition
- MUST show the prompt to the user before/after generation - they may be confused or offended by results
- See image-gen project files for context
- This is just the eligibility hook - full Replicate integration is a separate future piece

title: Pre-calculate skills matrix data in a scheduled command
description:
Currently the skills matrix calculates everything on-the-fly, including historical lookups via getSkillLevelAt() in the blade template.

Consider pre-computing the matrix data periodically (daily?) and storing it. Benefits:
- Removes method calls from blade templates
- Faster page loads (no N×M queries for users × skills)
- Enables more complex calculations without impacting UX
- Historical snapshots become trivial lookups

This is tentative - may not be needed if current performance is acceptable.

title: Add play/animate button to compact matrix time slider
description:
Add a play button next to the time slider that auto-animates from earliest date to now, looping through the history. Managers love animated charts.

STATUS: Partially implemented. The play button animates the slider position, but the grid cells do not update during playback. Manual slider dragging works correctly - cells transition and update as expected. The issue appears to be that the Alpine.js interval updating $wire.timelinePosition doesn't trigger Livewire to re-render the grid, even though the slider thumb moves.
close_reason: Closed
